items.title: Oil and semiconductor trade is reliant on chokepoints The 91% Problem Taiwan isn't just a security flashpoint, it's a single point of failure for the ...
items.description_html:
Oil and semiconductor trade is reliant on chokepoints

The 91% Problem

Taiwan isn't just a security flashpoint, it's a single point of failure for the global economy. 91% of the world's advanced chips are made there. A blockade could cut US GDP 5% and China's 9%.

Layer in Iran (China buys 80%+ of its shipped oil) and you see why neither side wants an unmanaged crisis.

The world's most critical supply chains, from oil to silicon, all run through a handful of chokepoints.

The takeaway: economies can stabilize even while the geopolitics stay unresolved.

items.description_html:
Everyone bent the rules for SpaceX. S&P DJI didn't.

Nasdaq, FTSE Russell and Morningstar CRSP all tore up the rulebook to fast-track it.

S&P didn't. It kept the 12-month seasoning, 10% float and four-quarter profit test.

SpaceX stays out of the S&P 500 for at least a year. Maybe much longer.

Choose your index wisely

items.title: Jefferies on Ambuja Cements (TP: ₹595) Post meeting management at the Adani Conference 1⃣Acknowledged recent underperformance; integration of Sanghi...
items.description_html:
Jefferies on Ambuja Cements (TP: ₹595)

Post meeting management at the Adani Conference

1⃣Acknowledged recent underperformance; integration of Sanghi, Penna & Orient has taken longer than expected.

2⃣ Next leg of value creation to come from execution, utilisation and cost efficiency, not aggressive capacity additions.

3⃣ Capacity to increase from 109 mtpa to 119 mtpa; further expansion on hold until returns improve. Earlier 140 MTPA target to be revisited.

4⃣ Reiterated cost-saving target of ~₹250/ton annually in FY27 & FY28, led by green power, logistics and operating efficiencies.

Jefferies says: Visible cost turnaround remains key to rebuilding investor confidence

@CNBCTV18Live

items.title: On World Environment Day today, India’s clean air challenge is no longer just about measuring pollution — it is about responding when the air turns ...
items.description_html:
On World Environment Day today, India’s clean air challenge is no longer just about measuring pollution — it is about responding when the air turns dangerous.

India can now measure its air in real time. The Sameer app and the National Air Quality Index have made pollution levels visible to citizens. But data alone does not protect people.

Urvashi Prasad and Palak Mahajan of Pahle India Foundation argue that India’s next clean air challenge is not another dashboard, but a health response system that moves when pollution levels rise.

That means clear protocols for schools, clinics, district administrations, frontline health workers and care programmes — especially for those most at risk.

https://www.downtoearth.org.in/environment/world-environment-day-india-can-measure-its-air-now-it-must-learn-how-to-respond

#WorldEnvironmentDay #AirPollution #PublicHealth #SameerApp #AQI #CleanAir #ClimateChange #India #EnvironmentalHealth #PahleIndiaFoundation

items.title: RBI policy goes smoothly. - No rate hike; no change in stance. Higher inflation forecast sets the stage for rate hikes starting August or October base...
items.description_html:
RBI policy goes smoothly.

- No rate hike; no change in stance. Higher inflation forecast sets the stage for rate hikes starting August or October based on.

- Full package of dollar inflow measures: Subsidised FCNR; overseas borrowings (don't understand why only PSUs though - should have been ownership agnostic)

-Capital gains removed for FPIs in debt, along with a widening of pool of securities with no investment limit restrictions.

- Exporters asked to bring back proceeds quicker (timeline had been unusually lengthened during the trade crisis)

[Not one to blow our own trumpet, but hugely relieved that Reuters has got key stories right - that dollar inflow measures are coming and that rate hikes are not (immediately). Despite pressure from competition and markets!]

https://www.reuters.com/world/asia-pacific/indian-central-bank-keeps-key-policy-rate-hold-despite-falling-currency-2026-06-05/

items.title: Big move by the Government + RBI to attract foreign capital, mainly into G-Secs 🔥 They are expanding the Fully Accessible Route by including all ne...
items.description_html:
Big move by the Government + RBI to attract foreign capital, mainly into G-Secs 🔥

They are expanding the Fully Accessible Route by including all new 15 year, 30 year & 40 year G-Sec issuances

At the same time, limits related to short term investment, concentration & individual securities for FPIs under the General Route are being removed

This basically makes it easier for foreign investors to participate in Indian Government bonds

Along with the tax benefits provided by the Government, these steps should help attract more foreign capital for Government borrowing

This is mainly a debt market focused move

src : RBI Governor statement

items.description_html:
RBI Goes Big To Get Dollars Into The Country !

Measures 👇

1. RBI is making it easier for foreign investors to buy Indian government bonds - How ? - All new 15y, 30y & 40y G-Secs will now be open to foreign investors.

2. ECBs raise by CPSEs - Subsidy on hedging cost will be borne by RBI (3-5 year ECBs). Has to be done by Sept 30.

3. FCNR (B) deposits - To be raised by authorised PSU banks - hedging cost subsidy taken care of bt RBI (3-5 year tenure). Has to be done by Sept 30.

How much can come in ? $20-30 billion ?

#RBI #Rupee #FII #Nifty #BankNifty #India @CNBCTV18News

Thermal comfort is not just about what the thermometer says.
Your body feels heat through a mix of four factors: air temperature, wind speed, exposure to sunlight and humidity.
On the 54th World Environment Day, we look at how stressful the outdoors has become — and why rising heat is not just a temperature story, but a public health concern.

Visual by @PulahaRoy

items.title: Bloomberg used indian prices to value rbi gold. Rbi hasnt changed to Indian prices yet , so they used lbma prices to value their gold and converted to...
items.description_html:
Bloomberg used indian prices to value rbi gold. Rbi hasnt changed to Indian prices yet , so they used lbma prices to value their gold and converted to rupees. Gold price in rupee went up 10% in may when the govt increased gold duty.

Since rbi releases the rupee and dollar value of gold every week, Bloomberg used to rupee value and Indian prices to figure out how much gold they have and assumed they've sold. Measure in dollars with Lbma prices, there would have been no sale detected.

Just explaining what's happened.

items.title: 🚨 Amazon pioneered e-commerce globally, but local startups like Flipkart were often ahead when it came to building an India-specific playbook. This...
items.description_html:
🚨 Amazon pioneered e-commerce globally, but local startups like Flipkart were often ahead when it came to building an India-specific playbook.

This included innovations such as cash on delivery (CoD), in-house logistics networks, and financing options like no-cost EMIs and debit card EMIs, many of which were popularised by Flipkart.

Amazon, however, has consistently proven to be a fast follower - quickly adapting successful ideas and scaling them aggressively.

A similar dynamic is now playing out in quick commerce, although it is still early days as Amazon gears up to challenge market leader Blinkit.

Amazon has reportedly committed more than $300 million to the business and has already opened 400–500 dark stores in key metros.

So why is quick commerce strategically important for Amazon? And what advantages, if any, does it have over Blinkit?

@AditiS90 has a detailed article

https://www.thearcweb.com/article/amazon-now-quick-commerce-blinkit-swiggy-instamart-zepto-XSr8g1JWxwreEr19

items.title: Emkay note says oil market will remains tight & accordingly adjusts their expectations for India! ⛽️ Brent may be ~25% off its peak on US-Iran deal ...
items.description_html:
Emkay note says oil market will remains tight & accordingly adjusts their expectations for India!

⛽️ Brent may be ~25% off its peak on US-Iran deal hopes, but the oil market remains fundamentally tight. Global inventories could approach critical levels by late Jun/early-Jul ’26, keeping upside risks alive.

What is Emkay expectations now?
-Raise FY27E Brent to $90/bbl vs $80
-Cut FY27 GDP growth to 6.3%
-Maintain inflation at 5.1%
-See CAD widening to 2.3% of GDP
-Bigger risk is external: BoP deficit >$75bn, amidst absent stronger capital inflows

Policy trade-offs will increasingly center on inflation, growth, FX stability & capital flows.

items.title: Inflation in Europe is rising rapidly: Eurozone Manufacturing PMI Input Prices spiked to 80 points in May, the highest since May 2022. This also marks...
items.description_html:
Inflation in Europe is rising rapidly:

Eurozone Manufacturing PMI Input Prices spiked to 80 points in May, the highest since May 2022.

This also marks the largest monthly increase in costs for firms over the last 4 years.

Furthermore, PMI Output Prices surged to 62 points, the highest in 3.5 years.

The rate companies are increasing the prices they charge for goods they produce has surged +12 points, or +24%, since the start of 2026.

This surge has been primarily driven by rising energy and raw material costs.

Meanwhile, supply chain delays are up to the highest level since the pandemic supply squeeze of 2022, adding further pressure on prices.

As a result, factories are forced to pass higher costs on to customers, which will push inflation even higher over the next few months.

Price pressures across Europe are accelerating.

items.title: BREAKING: Assets in US money market funds surged +$66 billion in the week ending May 28th, to a record $8.28 trillion. +$41 billion of that came in a ...
items.description_html:
BREAKING: Assets in US money market funds surged +$66 billion in the week ending May 28th, to a record $8.28 trillion.

+$41 billion of that came in a single day as investors rebalanced their portfolios ahead of month-end.

Year-to-date, money market funds have attracted +$172 billion in total inflows.

Since the 2020 pandemic, total assets have soared +112%, or +$4.4 trillion.

This comes as the market is pricing in a ~60% probability of a Fed rate hike this year, supporting demand for short-term cash-like assets.

Meanwhile, the 7-day average yield for US money market funds stood at 3.34% as of May 28th.

Investors are anticipating the Fed's next move.

items.title: US inflation is set to rise further: ISM Services Prices rose +0.6 points in May, to 71.3, the highest since August 2022. Since February, the index ha...
items.description_html:
US inflation is set to rise further:

ISM Services Prices rose +0.6 points in May, to 71.3, the highest since August 2022.

Since February, the index has risen +8.3 points, the biggest 3-month increase since 2021.

Diesel, gasoline, oil, and related commodities were the most frequently cited as "up in price" in the survey.

In May alone, no commodities were reported as "down in price."

Historically, rapidly rising services prices have led CPI inflation with a ~3-month lag.

The current reading suggests CPI could rise above 5.0% for the first time since early 2023, from the 3.8% seen in April.

Inflation pressures are mounting.

items.title: Chinese investors are dumping Hong Kong stocks at a record pace: Hong Kong equity ETFs listed in China posted a record -$3.7 billion in outflows last ...
items.description_html:
Chinese investors are dumping Hong Kong stocks at a record pace:

Hong Kong equity ETFs listed in China posted a record -$3.7 billion in outflows last week, the largest weekly outflow on record.

This marks the 5th consecutive weekly outflow and the 10th over the last 12 weeks.

By comparison, Hong Kong stock ETFs saw 11 weekly outflows during the entire 2025.

Capital is rotating into China-listed semiconductor and AI-linked stocks, which investors view as more direct beneficiaries of the AI boom.

Even a +10% surge in Tencent on Tuesday failed to stop the outflows, with mainland Chinese investors using the rally as an opportunity to exit ~$268 million worth of its shares during the same day.

Chinese investors are turning away from Hong Kong.

MicroStrategy, $MSTR, is now facing its biggest unrealized loss in history, at -$10.8 billion.

In other words, after 6 years of buying Bitcoin, the company is now down -17% on its position.

By comparison, the S&P 500 is up +116% over this same timeframe.

items.title: Nvidia is diverging from the rest of the chip sector: Nvidia, $NVDA, and the Semiconductor Index, $SOX, have moved in opposite directions on ~50% of a...
items.description_html:
Nvidia is diverging from the rest of the chip sector:

Nvidia, $NVDA, and the Semiconductor Index, $SOX, have moved in opposite directions on ~50% of all trading sessions over the last 50 trading days, near the highest frequency since the bull market started in 2022.

This percentage has more than quadrupled since the start of April.

Over this period, $SOX has surged +84% while $NVDA has risen +24%.

By comparison, $NVDA returned +277% while $SOX returned +70% from 2024 through 2025.

Throughout those 2 years, this metric fluctuated between 10% and 40%, when $NVDA was driving the sector higher.

The semiconductor rally has expanded well beyond Nvidia this year.

items.title: BREAKING: The S&P 500 ETF, $VOO, has officially become the first ETF in history to surpass $1 trillion in assets under management (AUM). $VOO has attr...
items.description_html:
BREAKING: The S&P 500 ETF, $VOO, has officially become the first ETF in history to surpass $1 trillion in assets under management (AUM).

$VOO has attracted +$69 billion in total inflows so far in 2026, on track for the largest annual intake since the fund’s inception in 2010.

This follows +$118 billion and +$138 billion recorded in 2024 and 2025, respectively.

Since the 2022 bear market, this fund’s AUM has more than TRIPLED.

By comparison, the AUM of S&P 500 ETFs, $IVV and $SPY, rose to ~$860 billion and ~$785 billion, respectively.

To put this into perspective, global ETF AUM stood at $21.9 trillion at the end of April, more than 3 times the $6.4 trillion held at the start of 2020, following 83 consecutive monthly inflows.

ETF investing has never been more popular.

items.description_html:
Conflict in the Backyard | Odisha
In Odisha’s Ganjam district, wild boars are changing the way farmers work their land.

Repeated crop raids have forced many to abandon paddy and shift to kewra cultivation. Some have declared crop holidays. Others are migrating in search of work.

Farmers say compensation for wildlife damage does not solve the problem when prevention measures such as fencing remain limited.

Even as they battle wild boars, monkeys and langurs, many in Ganjam continue to protect blackbuck, revered locally despite the crop losses they cause, writes Hrushikesh Mohanty

https://www.downtoearth.org.in/agriculture/conflict-in-the-backyard-in-odisha-wild-boars-push-farmers-from-paddy-to-kewra-cultivation

items.title: BREAKING: Venezuela’s crude oil exports have surged +61% YoY, to 1.25 million barrels per day, the highest in 7 years. The surge was led by rising sh...
items.description_html:
BREAKING: Venezuela’s crude oil exports have surged +61% YoY, to 1.25 million barrels per day, the highest in 7 years.

The surge was led by rising shipments to the US at ~558,000 barrels per day, India at ~427,000 barrels per day, and Europe at ~169,000 barrels per day.

In total, 67 cargoes were exported from Venezuelan ports in May.

Since November 2025, Venezuela’s oil exports have surged +150%, or ~750,000 barrels,

This comes as the US eased sanctions on Venezuela's oil industry following the capture of Maduro earlier this year.

Venezuela's oil ministry is now targeting output of 1.37 million barrels per day by year-end, which would mark a +22% increase from 2025 levels and the highest since US sanctions were first imposed in 2019.

Venezuelan oil is increasingly flowing into global markets.

In Himachal Pradesh, farmers say wild animals now cause more crop damage than floods or storms.

Monkeys, wild boars, nilgai, bears, peacocks and parrots are raiding fields and orchards, forcing many farmers to change crops, leave land fallow or spend nights guarding their fields.

items.title: India just did something global consumer internet hasn't seen before. Uber spent years building ride density in its first markets. Airbnb took nearly ...
items.description_html:
India just did something global consumer internet hasn't seen before.

Uber spent years building ride density in its first markets.
Airbnb took nearly a decade to scale its supply side.
DoorDash grew fast — but burned through years of subsidies to get there.

Zepto went from ₹0 to ₹40,000 crore annualised. In a few years. Without the same runway.
The difference isn't just speed. It's the underlying structure.
India gave Zepto something no other market could — a dense urban population with high purchase frequency, low average order gaps, and an appetite for convenience that compounds with every passing quarter.
That's not a growth hack. That's a structural tailwind.
This isn't India catching up to global consumer internet.

This is Zepto — and India — writing a playbook that didn't exist before.

items.description_html:
India’s big cat conservation story has drawn global attention. But success now brings new challenges.

@RaviChellam3 argues that the push to keep adding tiger reserves, and the obsession with “fortress conservation”, are not rooted in science or in India’s social, political and cultural realities.

With many tigers, lions and leopards living outside protected areas, India must recognise coexistence, protect habitats and make local communities partners in conservation.

items.description_html:
It's official.

MicroStrategy, $MSTR, is now facing its biggest unrealized loss in history, at -$10.8 billion.

In other words, after 6 years of buying Bitcoin, the company is now down -17% on its position.

By comparison, the S&P 500 is up +116% over this same timeframe.

Since MicroStrategy sold 32 Bitcoin at $77,135 per coin, their positions has lost -$11.8 billion in value.

This puts MicroStrategy's stock, $MSTR, down -77% since its record high.

Bear market is an understatement.

items.title: If you are investing directly in stocks, avoiding landmines is a must for survival The first step is to regularly track results And by results, it doe...
items.description_html:
If you are investing directly in stocks, avoiding landmines is a must for survival

The first step is to regularly track results

And by results, it does not only mean the income statement only

Cash flows and B/S are equally important, but they often do not get the attention they deserve

Many frauds & weak businesses start showing early signs in cash flows, receivables, debt, inventory, pledges, auditor remarks and poor disclosures

The second step is to take action when fundamentals deteriorate

If the business is weakening, cash flows are poor, disclosures are weak or management is not communicating clearly, exiting is always a better decision

Exit based on facts, rather than hope, attachment or emotion

A stock down 50% from your buying price can still fall another 50% And after that, it can fall another 50% again & so on

Every money saved is money earned

One simple rule which can be followed is to avoid companies that do not conduct concalls & don't have adequate disclosures

Yes, you may miss a few opportunities, but that is perfectly fine

Why invest in a company where management does not even want to regularly communicate with shareholders?

If disclosures are weak, management interaction is low & there is no clarity on business performance, conviction becomes very difficult

In direct stocks, survival is more important than chasing every opportunity

items.title: RT abey varghese: “To be conscious is an illness,” Fyodor Dostoevsky’s protagonist says in Notes from Underground, the satirical novella he wrote i...
items.description_html:
RT abey varghese
“To be conscious is an illness,” Fyodor Dostoevsky’s protagonist says in Notes from Underground, the satirical novella he wrote in response to 19th-century Russian nihilism. “I have many times tried to become an insect. But I was not equal even to that.” In 21st-century India, however, the roach is not a metaphor for running away from problems. Instead, it’s a symbol of survival — in the swamps of communalized politics, corruption, and staggering bureaucratic callousness, writes @andymukherjee70. https://www.bloomberg.com/opinion/articles/2026-06-03/india-s-cockroach-army-and-the-great-exam-betrayal?utm_source=website&utm_medium=share&utm_campaign=twitter

items.title: RT The Core: The RBI is more likely to explore ways to attract capital inflows than to tinker with the interest rates to take care of currency volatil...
items.description_html:
RT The Core
The RBI is more likely to explore ways to attract capital inflows than to tinker with the interest rates to take care of currency volatility, Chief Economist Rajani Sinha of @CareEdge_Group tells @govindethiraj on The Core Report.

"If the West Asia crisis lingers, we would definitely have an impact on growth. So the central bank has this difficult task of balancing inflation as well as taking care of the growth aspect."

The RBI’s Monetary Policy Committee will announce its decision on rates tomorrow.

👉 Key insights from the expert:

• Uncertainty surrounding the West Asia crisis and its impact on energy supplies will continue to impact financial markets, businesses, and consumer sentiment.

• Concerns over a weaker monsoon and its impact on food prices have heightened inflation risks.

• Raising interest rates is unlikely to significantly support the currency as the weak capital flows remain the core issue.

• FII outflows are common during crisis periods, but unlike before, the capital account was supported by stable FDI flows.

items.title: Another Day, Another PE looking to acquire stake in a hospital chain This is alarming for sure In the US - As of 2025, 488 US hospitals were owned by ...
items.description_html:
Another Day, Another PE looking to acquire stake in a hospital chain

This is alarming for sure

In the US -

As of 2025, 488 US hospitals were owned by private equity firms. That represents approximately 8.5% of all private hospitals in the U.S. and about 22–25% of all for-profit hospitals

In India -

PE ownership is highly concentrated in the organized corporate hospital sector, where many of the largest chains have PE, sovereign wealth fund, pension fund, or global institutional ownership:

Cloudnine – Temasek, TPG, Newquest; now another PE transaction is underway.
CARE Hospitals – Blackstone-backed.
Manipal Hospitals – Temasek-controlled.
HCG – CVC Capital-backed.
Sahyadri Hospitals – institutional ownership.
Ujala Cygnus, Sterling, KIMS Kerala, and several others have PE involvement.

The key distinction is:

In the U.S., PE ownership has penetrated thousands of healthcare assets and nearly a quarter of the for-profit hospital sector.

In India, PE ownership is still a relatively small percentage of the total hospital universe (India has tens of thousands of hospitals), but it is becoming increasingly dominant among the large corporate

I had met the management of a listed hospital at the Motilal conference, and they mentioned how US doctors are overworked, as PE-owned hospitals encourage them to perform more surgeries, unnecessary procedures, and medications to maximize sales and profits.

They run it like a business and not a hospital!

items.title: RT Nimesh Shah: CLSA on Kotak BK O-P. TP Rs 440 Management meet takeaways Kotak has identified four customer cohorts that it would like to focus on –...
items.description_html:
RT Nimesh Shah
CLSA on Kotak BK
O-P. TP Rs 440
Management meet takeaways
Kotak has identified four customer cohorts that it would like to focus on – affluent, middle-class, SME and corporate.
One change that has been brought about in bank is migration from product-level focus to customer-level focus
Idea is to look at a customer by the overall profitability they generate and not by a singular product approach.
Focus on technology clearly stood out, with an aim to reduce the cost of doing business over time.
While bank has reignited its focus on digital customers (‘811’), it will also focus on branch expansion
Felt that management was unwilling to sacrifice RoA for growth, despite its excess capital. It reiterated its usual loan growth guidance of 1.5-2x of nominal GDP.

UBS on Kotak BK
Buy TP Rs 500
Management meet takeaways
Focus on low-cost liability, retail loans and tech
Liability side to be supported by digital and branch
Tech to drive cost optimisation
On asset side, company continues to target 1.5-2x of nominal GDP growth with lower end likely in a weaker cycle & pursuing inorganic opportunities if required
Co would prioritise retail and MSME segments while remaining opportunistic in corporate loans & aspires to become third largest bank by group profit by 2030

items.title: RT Ananth Rupanagudi: Burrabazar (Bara Bazar), Calcutta— c. 1895. Burra Bazar (or Burrabazar) is a sprawling wholesale market in North Kolkata, traci...
items.description_html:
RT Ananth Rupanagudi
Burrabazar (Bara Bazar), Calcutta— c. 1895. Burra Bazar (or Burrabazar) is a sprawling wholesale market in North Kolkata, tracing its roots to the 18th century Sutanuti haat. Originally dominated by local Bengali merchants, it is now a bustling commercial nucleus and one of India’s largest wholesale markets, known globally for textiles, electronic goods, and festive shopping.

Long before British rule, the area was known as Sutanuti Haat, controlled by local indigenous traders like the Setts, Basaks, and wealthy merchants like Shobharam Basak, who supplied yarn to the East India Company.

During the mid-18th century, the market expanded significantly. It became deeply intertwined with the region's opium trade to China. Wealthy Marwari traders settled in the area, financing warehouses and introducing the Hundi (a traditional credit and bill-of-exchange system).

The area wasn't just a commercial hub; it drew diverse communities. For example, it housed one of Kolkata's earliest Jewish settlements and the Armenian community.

Burra Bazar remains the economic backbone of eastern India's wholesale trade. The labyrinth of narrow lanes is packed with specialized sub-markets, such as the Satyanarayan Market, dealing in clothes, jewelry, spices, and hardware.

Despite surviving massive fires and historical upheavals (including a 1756 fire set by Siraj-Ud-Daulah), it has retained its street-hierarchy layout and mercantile legacy as a core pillar of Kolkata's identity. #Kolkata #markets #history @KanchanGupta

items.title: RT Danny (Dennis) Citrinowicz ,داني سيترينوفيتش: "We Created a Monster" Iran’s reckless behavior over the last 24 hours, including its...
items.description_html:
RT Danny (Dennis) Citrinowicz ,داني سيترينوفيتش
"We Created a Monster"

Iran’s reckless behavior over the last 24 hours, including its threat to attack Israel if Israel strikes Beirut, is a direct consequence of how the recent war ended.

Tehran now feels far more confident in its position, believing it successfully withstood military pressure from what it views as the two most powerful militaries in the world: the United States and Israel.

This is not a temporary spike in rhetoric. It is part of a broader and more dangerous trend. Iran increasingly sees itself as the patron and protector of its regional proxies, extending what amounts to an informal security umbrella over groups such as Hezbollah and other members of the so-called “Axis of Resistance.” The threat against Israel over Beirut reflects this growing sense of ownership and responsibility for its partners across the region.

More importantly, this is further evidence of a profound shift in Iran’s decision-making process following the death of Supreme Leader Ali Khamenei. For decades, Khamenei was fundamentally cautious when it came to the direct use of Iranian military power, particularly when the risk of confrontation with the United States was involved. That restraint appears to be fading.

We are entering a new era in which Tehran feels stronger, more secure, and increasingly confident in the credibility of its deterrence. Iranian leaders now appear to believe that their willingness to absorb military pressure and survive it, has enhanced rather than weakened their strategic position.

The result is a more assertive Iran, a more dangerous Middle East, and a growing risk that future crises will escalate faster and further than anyone anticipated.

#iran
#IranWar‌


Ariel Oseran أريئل أوسيران: Iran’s Foreign Minister Abbas Araghchi tells Al-Mayadeen:

If Israel attacks Beirut, the result will be the return to war. Either the war stops in both Iran and Lebanon, or it doesn't stop neither in Iran nor in Lebanon.

Our armed forces are ready to launch devastating strikes

items.title: Another comeback that is taking place: Blackberry BlackBerry is back. Not with a phone. Not with a keyboard. With an OS quietly running inside 275 mil...
items.description_html:
Another comeback that is taking place: Blackberry

BlackBerry is back. Not with a phone. Not with a keyboard. With an OS quietly running inside 275 million cars, humanoid robots, and surgical systems and now partnered with NVIDIA for the age of Physical AI. Here's the full story. 👇

2. In 2008, BlackBerry was worth ~$75 billion that is more than NVIDIA, Amazon, Netflix, and Starbucks combined at that time. It owned 50% of the US smartphone market. The "CrackBerry" was the status symbol of the era. Obama refused to give his up. Wall Street ran on it.

3. Then came the iPhone. And BlackBerry's co-CEOs did something fatal, they called the touchscreen "a gimmick." They doubled down on the physical keyboard and enterprise email while the world moved on. They missed touchscreen UX, failed to build an app ecosystem, and prioritized enterprise over a consumer revolution that was happening right in front of them.

4. The fall was brutal. By 2016, BlackBerry's smartphone market share was literally 0.0%. Revenue cratered, the stock fell 96% from its peak, and by 2018 they had exited the phone business entirely. Most investors wrote the obituary and moved on.

5. What almost everyone missed: buried inside this wreck of a phone company was a software gem they'd quietly acquired in 2010 — QNX. For years it sat ignored while the phone business burned. But QNX was doing something remarkable: becoming the nervous system of modern automobiles.

6. QNX is a Real-Time Operating System (RTOS) — the foundational software layer for safety-critical systems where failure is not an option. The touchscreen in your car runs Android Automotive. But when your car senses danger at 120 km/h and fires the emergency brakes? That's QNX. It's not the face. It's the backbone.

7. The moat has three layers. First — regulatory lock-in. QNX OS for Safety is certified to ISO 26262 ASIL-D, the absolute highest functional safety standard in automotive. Getting a competitor to this level takes years and hundreds of millions. It's not just a technical moat, it's a regulatory moat. Apple tried to build a competing car OS, spent $10 billion, and quietly killed the project. That tells you everything about the barrier to entry.

8. Second layer is the automotive adoption cycles. When an OEM bakes QNX into a vehicle platform, it stays for the life of that platform which is often 7 to 10 years. Switching costs are astronomical. Recertification from scratch is unthinkable. BMW just selected QNX for its next-generation Neue Klasse platform. That's a decade of locked, recurring revenue from one design win.

9. Third layer is 45 years of safety-critical DNA. QNX was founded in 1980. Its microkernel architecture, where safety and non-critical processes are completely memory-isolated, took decades to perfect. This isn't software you build in a sprint. It's institutional knowledge accumulated over nearly half a century.

10. The metric that matters most is the royalty backlog — locked future revenue from already-signed contracts. In FY2022 it stood at $460M Today it's ~$950M. A 2x jump in four years, providing revenue visibility. This is predictable, high-margin, recurring software revenue and the market is still waking up to it.

11. Now comes the AI angle most investors are sleeping on. The world is moving fast toward what QNX calls "Physical AI" machines that don't just compute, they perceive, reason, and act in the real world. Humanoid robots. Surgical systems. Industrial automation. Autonomous vehicles. Every single one needs software that is deterministic, real-time, and safety-certified. That's QNX's exact domain.

12. The NVIDIA partnership is the key signal. In August 2025, QNX integrated with NVIDIA DRIVE AGX Thor for autonomous vehicles. Then in April 2026 at Hannover Messe, they deepened it, QNX OS for Safety 8.0 + NVIDIA IGX Thor + Halos Safety Stack for targeting robotics, medical tech, and industrial automation. BB stock jumped 11% on the TSX the day of the announcement. NVIDIA is the dominant AI hardware platform. QNX is now its safety OS layer. Think Windows for physical AI.

13. Already deployed across 24 of the world's top 25 EV manufacturers. Inside BMW, Mercedes, Audi, Toyota, Tata, and Mahindra. Demonstrated inside humanoid robots at Embedded World 2026 and surgical robotic arms at CES 2026. About 20% of QNX revenue now comes from non-automotive sectors and that share is growing fast.

14. This isn't just a BlackBerry story. There's a mega-theme in 2026: dot-com era giants are having AI-era revivals. Cisco went from $800M in AI orders to $9B projected in FY2026, and its stock surged 17% in a single day, its best day since 2002. Intel, Dell, and Corning are all seeing the same dynamic.

The companies that built the original infrastructure of the internet are now becoming the infrastructure of the AI age.

15. Q4 FY2026 numbers confirm the turnaround. Total revenue hit $156M (+10% YoY). QNX revenue: $78.7M (+20% YoY). Full-year QNX revenue: $268M (+14%). Gross margin: 78.2% and expanding. Adjusted EBITDA for the full year: $107.1M (+27%). Operating cash flow: $45.6M

GAAP net income: $53.2M which is the 8th consecutive quarter of improving profitability.

The royalty backlog: ~$950M. The CEO said it plainly: "The turnaround is complete."

16. FY2027 guidance: revenue of $584M–$611M, QNX alone at $290M–$307M. The company sits on $432M in cash with a share buyback authorized. Key growth triggers: QNX Alloy Core (won CES 2026 Best in Show), BMW Neue Klasse ramp, robotics pipeline with Boston Dynamics and Agility Robotics, NATO-accredited government contracts, and a growing developer ecosystem with 80 universities building on QNX globally.

17. The final word. "On a car, you'll never see QNX's logo. What you will see is a better experience." That invisibility is the thesis. The best infrastructure businesses don't scream for attention. They become indispensable. QNX is the TCP/IP of physical AI.

Not investment advice. Do your own research.

items.title: 1 GW greenfield nuclear power plant requires 4,000–5,000 tons of pipe spools. The current pipeline of plants both domestically and internationally is...
items.description_html:
1 GW greenfield nuclear power plant requires 4,000–5,000 tons of pipe spools.

The current pipeline of plants both domestically and internationally is large enough that RFSS would stay booked just on new greenfield projects alone, without needing replacement demand to sustain volumes.

2047 vision of 100 GW of nuclear capacity as the long-term demand driver.

To meet the broader power demand thermal and renewable alone won't suffice, especially with the data center buildout accelerating globally.

RFSS is currently the only NPCIL-approved pipe spooling facility from India. This is a meaningful competitive moat given nuclear qualification timelines. Beyond NPCIL, they've now secured international approvals for nuclear projects in Egypt, Turkey, and Hungary expanding the TAM beyond domestic.

Current RFSS order book stands at ~550 crores, with ₹480–500 crores expected to convert into revenue in FY27 itself (guidance: 20–25% growth over FY26s 390 crores). The active bidding pipeline is $400–500 million

Pipe spool orders are placed roughly 1–1.5 years after land acquisition for a nuclear plant. Delivery timelines range from 6 to 18 months post-order. So from land acquisition to spool delivery, you're looking at a 1.5–3 year lag

Current RFSS margins are running at 30–35%. Sanghvi guided that near-term margins look sustainable at these levels, but over time should normalize to 20–25% as the business scales and competition potentially enters.

Disclaimer - not a buy/sell recommendation

items.description_html:
Which battery chemicals will be required as Battery production scales up

China’s EV battery demand rose sharply from around 16GWh in 2015 to nearly 770GWh by 2025, showing how quickly demand can scale once electric vehicle or EV penetration accelerates.

If India follows a similar path with a lag, battery demand could reach around 200GWh by 2032F.

This would create demand for nearly 300,000t of cathode material, 180,000t of anode material, 200,000–240,000t of electrolytes, 26,000–36,000t of LiPF₆ and 160,000–200,000t of solvents.

items.title: Ferrochrome situation in SA - South Africa negotiated a 62 cents/kWh power tariff for ferrochrome down from 136 cents standard rate. Will this restart...
items.description_html:
Ferrochrome situation in SA -

South Africa negotiated a 62 cents/kWh power tariff for ferrochrome down from 136 cents standard rate.

Will this restart SA production and flood the market? Unlikely.

More SA ferrochrome → less chrome ore to China → lower Chinese production. Chrome units stay broadly neutral globally.

The real question isn't if SA restarts it's whether 62c is sustainable long-term. Glencore says it's survival pricing. If ferrochrome prices crack, even this tariff falls apart.

Ultimate resilience is not shaped through policy interventions but through strong fundamentals.

Source - IMFA Q4 Concall

Disclaimer - not a recommendation to buy/sell

items.title: Shankar bhai, net imports is the only way to judge reserves, and let me explain why. We pay for imports with a lag, and we receive export proceeds wit...
items.description_html:
Shankar bhai, net imports is the only way to judge reserves, and let me explain why. We pay for imports with a lag, and we receive export proceeds with a lag. Assuming that we will not get any export proceeds, while having to pay for imports is extreme overkill.

Also remember that a lot of imports are used to then export. Which means we would have imported some crude oil to refine and send out petrol/diesel. This happens every month, and payments and receipts happen every month. Assuming exports stop, then that means corresponding imports also stop suddenly and do not need to be paid for, and whatever imports are already in transit is going be paid for by whatever imports are in transit on the outward direction.

Similarly, exports of services happen and don't just stop. They are independent in some way.

The IMF methodology is wrong. We all know it, because of how different it works in reality, even in a crisis. And we have seen many crisis areas, net imports are the only real mechanism on which to judge reserves, not gross. The dogma that oh my god IMF is right because, well because it's IMF, is very wrong.

In the simple example, things dont happen in a vacuum. You don't import first and then export. You export things you make or haev already, including services. At the same time that you're importing. The practical example is that if you import $100 in a month and export $50 on what you already have produced, then you get the $50 at about the same time that you have to pay the $100, so you just need $50. At a country level, you do not assume that every single export fails to receive anything, while every single import has to be paid in full. It just is not practical.

And no, we didn't sell gold. And we've sold enough of our TBonds, and will continue to if FPIs keep selling.


Shankar Sharma: Owing to the limits on word count, a few important things were left out from the original version:

What most people don't know probably is that our FDI is not the way it is perceived: around 70-75% of it is VC PE money!

By no means can that be called stable capital . It is

Varun Beverages' commentary on Market Competitive Intensity –

"Competition is there, but there is enough market for everybody to take, as we have said every time. We are adding close to half a million and maybe more chilling equipment, which is between Campa, Coke and us, and plus the individual outlets are buying 4-5L own coolers.

So, there is a million chilling equipment, refrigerators going in the market on a year-to-year basis. That is expanding the market drastically. We think whoever does a good go-to-market and whoever can expand their distribution will win the game.

We are trying to do that and are expanding at about 300,000 - 400,000 outlets every year. Hopefully, this year we might expand to half a million outlets. And we think that is what is giving us the growth."

items.title: RT Rajeev Mantri: Indian business is controlled by a small coterie of business dynasties, many of whom are linked together by marriage. It is a closed...
items.description_html:
RT Rajeev Mantri
Indian business is controlled by a small coterie of business dynasties, many of whom are linked together by marriage. It is a closed circle of elites, who function like old school zamindars.

One simply doesn’t expect these 21st century zamindars to be able to contribute to Indian economy’s growth to $20T in output. In fact, the Licence Raj orientation and manipulative, predatory mindset of many companies is a serious impediment to India’s progress.

For India to progress, these businesses have to be out-competed and neutered. The same had happened 30 years ago, when the first flush of reforms speared through and reshaped the Indian business landscape. Many of today’s corporate giants swept away blue chip names of that era - as life goes, today the challengers of that period have become like the old guard they had displaced.

The problem is barriers to entry and barriers to scaling slow down the turnover. This generation of Indians is counting on Prime Minister @narendramodi to deliver on necessary reforms, so that new entrants can once again displace the distracted, tired and corpulent incumbents who at this point can be generously described as squatters impeding the path of India’s progress. https://x.com/rmantri/status/2054118137986269360?s=46&t=N3l76pbsGLWSFxlrfMXdmQ


Uday Kotak: Google which is cash surplus, just announced an additional capital raise of $80 bn.
Google annual profit is $160 bn, last quarter $62 bn, and market cap $4.5 trillion. That is close to total profits and market cap of all Indian listed companies put together.
It’s a wake up call

items.description_html:
RT Sridhar Vembu
Karuvi update: these are the major issues that come up in power tools design. At 11000+ rpm, does it overheat, does it vibrate too much and does it produce too much noise?

We have to find parts that work well and are also price competitive. Low end Chinese power tools won't pass all the tests but they are very cheap. We decided we have to pass all the tests the Japanese and German tools would pass.

Finally we have the product that meets that quality bar shipping to trial customers locally.

Tooling costs are another major issue. We are planning a tool room.

All this has taught us a lot of lessons. We will roll out a full line of tools later this year.

To build anything that truly lasts, it takes time!

items.title: RBI May Have Sold Gold Amid Rising Capital Outflows: Abhishek Gupta, Senior India Economist at Bloomberg Economics, estimates that the RBI sold gold w...
items.description_html:
RBI May Have Sold Gold Amid Rising Capital Outflows:
Abhishek Gupta, Senior India Economist at Bloomberg Economics, estimates that the RBI sold gold worth around $12 billion in the two weeks ended May 22 while purchasing approximately $7.5 billion in foreign-currency assets.
The assessment is based on publicly available reserve data and comes at a time when rising crude oil prices and persistent capital outflows are weighing on India's external position.
According to Gupta, the decline in gold holdings occurred despite a recent increase in import duties on the precious metal, a factor that would normally support the value of the RBI's bullion reserves. This, he says, points to the possibility that the central bank was actively selling gold

items.title: Index funds in the US have to buy SpaceX, as nasdaq prepares to add it to the Nasdaq 100 in 15 days after listing, around July 7. They changed the rul...
items.description_html:
Index funds in the US have to buy SpaceX, as nasdaq prepares to add it to the Nasdaq 100 in 15 days after listing, around July 7. They changed the rules to allow fast track listing of any co thats in the top 40 by full market cap (usually 3 months, and nasdaq doesn't do free float, it uses full market Cap)

Two important changes to the index inclusion concept:

1. Companies with less than 10% free float can now list. This wasn't allowed earlier and spacex will have around 4% free float when it lists.

2. Normally a 3m curing period is needed formipps to be included. It's now just 15 trading days.

Both these allow Spacex to.get on to the Nasdaq index. The weight is limited to 3x it's free float which is like 0.5% of the index. This is not much at all to start with.

Other indexes too will eventually list the company. The free float will expand dramatically between 70 days and 180 days after listing, as shares get out of their lock in periods, at which time it's weight in the index will increase substantially.

The big deal will only be in that time frame. Every index rebalance, will increase spacex weight which means index funds will reduce other stocks and increase spacex exposure, just as shares get unlocked.

In some sense, this means that if we expect any damage due to spacex lockups opening, some of it will be absorbed by index funds and etfs. This helps the stock, of course, and perhaps the market.

Markets usually top with large ipos because the ipos suck out liquidity. That could happen but many months after the ipo, as unlocked investors sell and at the same time, spacex will need to raise more. It's an interesting thing that other ai companies too are planning to list soon. Perhaps they get it, and want to get their raises done before all this unfolds. Or perhaps it's just that they want in on this ride.

Given that passive is a substantial part of the market now, it's an easy choice to move the decision makers of indices. Think about it, a stock called pp waterballs could list at a market cap of 2 trillion with 4% free float, sold to friendly investment bankers, and between 15 days and 180 days, a large portion of the free float could be sold to index funds and index huggers.

Spacex might be revolutionary but it doesn't really have to be. Index creators are the new fund managers.

https://indexes.nasdaqomx.com/docs/Nasdaq-100_Index_Consultation_February_2026_Summary_of_Responses_and_Conclusion.pdf

1. Gross margins are expanding

3. Historical track record is stellar
4. Company is doing pain today for gain tomorrow activity

These are unique set ups where investors discard these companies due to optically low profitability, and given the fact that these businesses are under earning.

Today's pain, ends up becoming tomorrow's gain when opex phase ends. Which is when we get operating leverage effect

items.title: Writing is hard. Good writing is even harder. Most readers only see the finished article. They don't see the false starts, discarded drafts, changing ...
items.description_html:
Writing is hard. Good writing is even harder.

Most readers only see the finished article. They don't see the false starts, discarded drafts, changing conclusions, or the months spent wrestling with an idea before it becomes coherent enough to publish.

Writing about a multi-bagger after it has already arrived is relatively easy. Writing about one while it is still going through debt, dilution, weak ROCE, negative free cash flow and years of investor frustration is a very different exercise.

Living through the pain, while continuing to think independently, requires a different temperament altogether.

That is why I often say that investing is not just about analysing businesses. It is about understanding time, uncertainty and human behaviour, especially your own.

Attached is a small extract from a piece I've been working on.

items.description_html:
JPM's Sajjid Chinoy in his pre-policy note writes:

"The RBI is likely to reiterate the “separability” principle under the inflation-targeting regime: Policy rates are used to manage growth-inflation dynamics, while FX volatility is addressed through FX reserves and other regulatory measures. Since the adoption of the inflation-targeting framework in 2014, the RBI has remained consistent with this separability principle."

items.title: Jeena Sikho Lifecare’s management has laid out an aggressive growth roadmap. Current sales: ~₹800 crore 3–5 year sales target: ~₹3,000 crore PAT t...
items.description_html:
Jeena Sikho Lifecare’s management has laid out an aggressive growth roadmap.

Current sales: ~₹800 crore
3–5 year sales target: ~₹3,000 crore
PAT target: 4x–5x in 4 years
Bed capacity target: 7,000–10,000 beds in 3–5 years

But the bigger trigger could be insurance.

Current revenue per occupied bed is around ₹4,650/day.
New insurance agreements are being signed at ~₹8,800–₹9,200 per bed/day.

Ayushman Yojana draft rates are also around ₹8,800–₹9,400/day.

That means higher insurance coverage can materially improve realization per bed.

Management also said wellness treatments are being covered under insurance, and the company is in talks with insurers to route select disease cases to Jeena Sikho, where treatment can be delivered more cost-effectively.

The model is simple:

More beds + higher insurance coverage + recurring medicine revenue = operating leverage.

Execution will decide the story.


Beat The Street: Jeena Sikho Lifecare’s unit economics look interesting

A 100-bed facility typically needs ₹2.5–3.5 cr capex — just ₹3–4 lakh per bed.

Monthly fixed cost includes ₹50–55 lakh payroll + ₹10–12 lakh rent.

items.title: So it's fashionable to quote archaic economic concepts like oh we have only 6 months of imports. Imports don't happen in a vacuum. We have exports. We...
items.description_html:
So it's fashionable to quote archaic economic concepts like oh we have only 6 months of imports.

Imports don't happen in a vacuum. We have exports. We have remittances (which is effectively people we export). Without them, we will have much much lower imports.

Our Current Account Deficit is $50 bn in FY2025. It might be $80 bn in FY2026. (We will know by end of June) To give you an idea, for the first nine months - Apr to Dec - it adds up to $30bn.

We have about $550bn of currency reserves. That's around 7 years of this enhanced deficit, and another $100bn in Gold. Most of the drop has come because of FPI outflows that involved selling dollars. And we do that because we have ALWAYS bought dollars on the way in, to build these insanely high reserves in the first place.

We have 7 years of net imports. It's not 6 months. It's not one year. Assuming all exports stop is stupid if you also assume that imports keep happening with zero exports.

Sure, it's also fashionable to blame SIPs. But we need to list more companies so that our SIPs can go into more avenues - expand the amount of free float available. Whether it's the Tata Sons, a Jio or an NSE, new listings will allow the market to absorb more capital - and it might still hurt the market in the short term.

Some stocks rise because of relative illiquidity of their free floats. This is often harnessed by a lot of equity investors and sometimes, big stories are made because companies (or large investors) are able to control the amount of stock actually available to trade. Even our very liquid stock options can be easily moved by moving the underlying stocks using relatively small capital - Jane street showed that to us by doing it with even small amounts like 5000 cr. IIRC.

If our stock market gets more liquid, we will probably see less crazy growth on just the back of SIPs, more on earnings growth. And it's possible that markets correct in the short term - any large IPO has, in the past, preceded corrections, from RPL to RPOWER to the bunch in 2021/22 and so on. Some of this is attributable to simply the movement of liquidity and availability of more options to invest.

Side note: The US is going to see large IPOs now. Make of that what you will.

The solution to free flow is free flow. The RBI is the primary provider of liquidity to the dollar-rupee market, it shouldn't be, but for that we should comprehensively change FEMA, otherwise it simply needs to sell when FPIs sell. FPIs can and will continue to sell until there is demand for India at the prices they want; and this notion that we can stop them from selling by tanking our markets so much that they give up on us, is such a losing proposition for everyone. Sure, it worked in the 90s, but a lot of ridiculous stuff worked in the 90s that doesn't work now.

I do agree that people should build more businesses. And that is happening, though the people that create them aren't from those that have truckloads of money. We're seeing some of them succeed now, and the next set of businesses will be financed by those enriched by their successes.

We could also go back to pagers because holy crow you people doomscroll too much. That's just walking backwards. I just want to say things aren't as bad as they seem, and you have to muddle through the bad times to see yourself into the good ones.

items.title: RT Ananth Rupanagudi: 1865 Elephanta Island, Bombay - picnic inside the Elephanta Caves. In about 1865, British colonial elites in Bombay began a cont...
items.description_html:
RT Ananth Rupanagudi
1865 Elephanta Island, Bombay - picnic inside the Elephanta Caves. In about 1865, British colonial elites in Bombay began a controversial tradition of holding lavish picnics inside the ancient Elephanta Caves. These rock-cut shrines, dedicated to Lord Shiva, became backdrops for western leisure, featuring catered meals and servants within the sacred temple pillars.

During the mid-19th century, picnicking inside indigenous religious sites became a popular but highly controversial display of British colonial leisure. This photo features a group of Europeans enjoying a picnic surrounded by the towering stone carvings of the Kalyanasundara (the marriage of Shiva and Parvati) in Cave 1.

While treated by the British as exotic sightseeing and an extension of Romantic-era leisure, many indigenous groups felt the events were deeply disrespectful to native religious beliefs.

The 1865 event set a precedent for later royal visits to the island. Later that century, the tradition evolved into grand "Royal Picnics". The Duke of Edinburgh and later the Prince of Wales were hosted for exclusive dinner parties and receptions inside the caves.

Historians view these lavish gatherings not just as casual outings, but as calculated stagings of British imperial power in the region. Today, the Elephanta Caves are a protected UNESCO World Heritage Site. #Mumbai #colonialpower #heritage

items.title: RT SOIC Research: Today, let's talk about a SaaS business that most people would dismiss at first glance. An IT company. A software business. In 2025-...
items.description_html:
RT SOIC Research
Today, let's talk about a SaaS business that most people would dismiss at first glance.

An IT company. A software business. In 2025-26, the automatic reaction is, "AI will eat these guys alive."
And honestly? For most SaaS companies, that fear is valid.

But this one is different.

This isn't a generic software vendor selling dashboards or CRM tools. This business has quietly built itself into the invisible infrastructure of a $1.5 trillion global travel industry.

Every time you book a hotel on Booking. com, every time an airline adjusts its fare, every time a hotel decides how much to spend on Google ads there's a high chance this company's technology is running in the background, making that decision happen.

And here's the contrarian thesis that we'll break down in this thread:

AI doesn't disrupt this business. AI makes its moat deeper.

While the market debates whether SaaS companies will survive the AI wave, this one is already deploying AI agents that deliver 300% revenue lifts for customers, has built the world's largest travel intent data platform with 1.5 billion data points, and is in active conversations with OpenAI and Google to become the advertising infrastructure layer for AI-powered travel.

So in this thread, we're going to break it all down for you in simple terms:

→ What the business actually does (and why it can't be replicated)
→ Why financial metrics of this company are among the best in Indian SaaS
→ What's going to drive the next phase of growth
→ The real risks that you should watch
→ And the big question, is AI a threat or the biggest tailwind this business has ever had?

items.title: RT SOIC Research: Today, let's talk about a SaaS business that most people would dismiss at first glance. An IT company. A software business. In 2025-...
items.description_html:
RT SOIC Research
Today, let's talk about a SaaS business that most people would dismiss at first glance.

An IT company. A software business. In 2025-26, the automatic reaction is, "AI will eat these guys alive."
And honestly? For most SaaS companies, that fear is valid.

But this one is different.

This isn't a generic software vendor selling dashboards or CRM tools. This business has quietly built itself into the invisible infrastructure of a $1.5 trillion global travel industry.

Every time you book a hotel on Booking. com, every time an airline adjusts its fare, every time a hotel decides how much to spend on Google ads there's a high chance this company's technology is running in the background, making that decision happen.

And here's the contrarian thesis that we'll break down in this thread:

AI doesn't disrupt this business. AI makes its moat deeper.

While the market debates whether SaaS companies will survive the AI wave, this one is already deploying AI agents that deliver 300% revenue lifts for customers, has built the world's largest travel intent data platform with 1.5 billion data points, and is in active conversations with OpenAI and Google to become the advertising infrastructure layer for AI-powered travel.

So in this thread, we're going to break it all down for you in simple terms:

→ What the business actually does (and why it can't be replicated)
→ Why financial metrics of this company are among the best in Indian SaaS
→ What's going to drive the next phase of growth
→ The real risks that you should watch
→ And the big question, is AI a threat or the biggest tailwind this business has ever had?

items.title: RT Nithin Kamath: While everybody is busy stuffing an AI chatbot into every product, the folks at @Tijori1 are using AI to build high-quality market r...
items.description_html:
RT Nithin Kamath
While everybody is busy stuffing an AI chatbot into every product, the folks at @Tijori1 are using AI to build high-quality market research tools. They’re calling it Tijori Stack, and what they are doing is taking the messy and scattered universe of company disclosures, financials, filings, concalls, and regulatory documents, and extracting useful insights.

They have several products:

1. Concall Monitor

Tracking concalls is annoying because the transcript comes after a few days. Then there’s the fact that you have to sit through one hour of management-speak just to find the three useful things they said. Tijori is trying to fix that.

With Concall Monitor, a few minutes after a concall ends, you get the full transcript and an AI-generated summary that extracts the key insights like pricing, margins, growth, demand, guidance, capex, risks, etc.
But the interesting thing is not just summarization but the management consistency check. Tijori tracks what management has said historically vs. what they are saying now. If a company promised something four quarters ago, the system can help track whether they followed through, changed the story, postponed the target, or pretended the earlier comment never happened.

2. Report on Demand

Tijori ingests pretty much all the filings and disclosures that a company makes, from financials, concalls, exchange filings, and regulatory disclosures to other company-specific documents. Using this, they generate different kinds of company reports:

Risk Probe Report gives you all the red flags and key risks.

Management Credibility Report looks at what management said versus what they actually did.

Five-year revenue and EBITDA estimate is an AI-assisted financial forecast grounded in company data.

All these reports are useful when you are doing first-pass research on a company. If you are tracking five companies and want to quickly figure out which are bad and which are worth researching further, these reports help. Instead of reading annual reports, concalls, exchange filings, presentations, and financials from scratch, you can use these reports to build a baseline view in minutes.

3. Radar

Radar is probably my favourite idea in the stack.

It allows you to define a metric or risk you care about for a particular company, and Tijori keeps scanning company disclosures and financials for relevant mentions. When the metric you define shows up, you get an alert.

And the metric does not have to be a typical financial metric.

You can track things like client concentration, employee growth, dollar revenue exposure, margin pressure, working capital stress, promoter pledging, regulatory risk, capex delays, inventory build-up, receivables, raw material pressure, or anything else that matters to your thesis. This is powerful because every investor has a different question.

Radar turns those questions into live monitors. I don’t think there are many products in India, or even in the world, that do this well.

4. Atlas

When you use ChatGPT or Claude to ask questions about a listed company, the answer is based on info that scraped from the public web. This is problematic because public web data about companies is messy.

There is news, commentary, rumours, stale articles, bad research takes, and gossip. Sometimes it is useful, but often it is noise. What makes Atlas different is that it gives you answers that are grounded in company-specific material like filings, disclosures, financials, concalls, and other company data.

Instead of asking a generic chatbot, “Tell me about this company,” and getting an unreliable answer scraped from the internet, you are asking a system that is constrained by the company’s own disclosures and financial history. This ensures there are no hallucinations.

items.title: RT The Indian Express: The Maharashtra government on May 13 exempted an iron ore mining and processing project in Gadchiroli from wildlife clearance b...
items.description_html:
RT The Indian Express
The Maharashtra government on May 13 exempted an iron ore mining and processing project in Gadchiroli from wildlife clearance by claiming — incorrectly — it was not located in any tiger corridor.

The project proposal involves diverting 9.4 sq km of forest land in Gadchiroli for iron ore extraction and processing by Lloyds Metals & Energy.

It received forest clearance on April 15 and environment clearance on May 12, a day before it was exempted from obtaining wildlife clearance.

According to the project site map submitted by Lloyds to the Ministry of Environment, Forest and Climate Change, the area falls in forest compartments 196, 197, 273, 274, 275, 276, 298, 300 and 301.

All but one (300) of these compartments are identified as part of the Tadoba-Indravati tiger corridor in the National Tiger Conservation Authority (NTCA)-approved Tiger Conservation Plan (TCP) of the Tadoba Andhari tiger reserve.

items.title: RT Ananth Rupanagudi: The old pontoon bridge that was later replaced by the Howrah Bridge, Caclutta (Kolkata) — 1902. The original Howrah Pontoon Bri...
items.description_html:
RT Ananth Rupanagudi
The old pontoon bridge that was later replaced by the Howrah Bridge, Caclutta (Kolkata) — 1902. The original Howrah Pontoon Bridge was a floating bridge over the Hooghly River linking Calcutta (Kolkata) and Howrah. Designed by Sir Bradford Leslie, it was assembled in India from parts built in England and opened on October 17, 1874. It featured a removable center section to allow boats to pass through.

By the early 1900s, enormous increases in traffic and the limitations of a floating bridge during extreme weather led officials to seek a sturdier alternative.

Planning for a new bridge was delayed by World War I. Construction of the modern cantilever bridge (now known as Rabindra Setu) began in 1936. It was constructed with 23,000 tons of steel joined by over 2 million rivets instead of nuts and bolts. The new iconic bridge opened to the public on February 3, 1943. #Kolkata #bridge #history

items.title: RT Boring_Business: Berkshire Hathaway just announced they are acquiring Taylor Morrison (TMHC) for $72.5/share, or an $8.5B enterprise value. The imp...
items.description_html:
RT Boring_Business
Berkshire Hathaway just announced they are acquiring Taylor Morrison (TMHC) for $72.5/share, or an $8.5B enterprise value. The implied premium is roughly 24% on latest share price

The multiple for the deal is cheap at ~9.3x LTM earnings or ~8.8x adjusted earnings. On a book value basis, Berkshire is paying ~1.2x book. Historical homebuilders have transacted at 1.0-1.5x book value, so this is on the lower end of the range

Interesting deal for a few reasons
> Clayton Homes, which is currently owned by Berkshire, has seen sales slide downwards because of high interest rates. New CEO Greg Abel called this out directly at 2026 shareholder meeting. They have to believe that mortgage rates are at or near its peak for this new deal to make sense

> During the same annual meeting, they had explicitly mentioned that Berkshire would try to consolidate their homebuilding assets into a combined platform. This includes Clayton Homes, MiTek, Bejamin Moore, Acme Brick, Shaw etc. They also own the brokerage side through Berkshire HomeServices. TMHC fits into the picture by supplementing these existing platforms. This is also the first major acquisition by Greg Abel after succeeding Warren Buffett

A very basic Google search + simple math will tell you most PMS products are just exclusivity wrapped in smart-sounding jargon.

Heavy fees.
Frequent underperformance.
“Smart money” marketing.
Luxury positioning.

The business model is brilliant for fund managers.

In many cases, PMS is simply a very sophisticated way of transferring wealth from investors’ pockets to fund managers’ pockets.

The richer the narrative gets, the poorer the investor often becomes.

Retail investors think they’re buying alpha.

Most are just buying expensive storytelling.

items.description_html:
RT Ministry of Culture
This 4,500-year-old terracotta dice from the Indus-Saraswati Civilization is a powerful reminder of India’s living heritage. Dicing is also mentioned as a popular game in Rig and Atharva Vedas (two of the four sacred Vedic scriptures).

From symbols and craftsmanship to rituals, yogic practices, and collective memory, numerous elements of ancient Indian civilization continue to thrive in the daily social and religious life of Indian society across regions and communities.

Civilizational inheritance is not just about geography or ruins, it is defined by living customs, symbols, rituals, and unbroken cultural consciousness. India is the enduring living continuity of the Indus-Saraswati Civilization.

#IndusSaraswatiCivilization #AncientIndianHeritage

items.title: In Atomic Habits, James Clear writes: "The more immediate pleasure you get from an action, the more strongly you should question whether it aligns wit...
items.description_html:
In Atomic Habits, James Clear writes: "The more immediate pleasure you get from an action, the more strongly you should question whether it aligns with your long-term goals."

A good investing example is the technology boom of the late 1990s. Many investors abandoned diversified portfolios and rushed into internet stocks because the gains were immediate and exciting. Every day seemed to bring another story of someone doubling their money. The pleasure was instant. The validation was constant.

Meanwhile, a more disciplined investor who continued buying broad market index funds or stocks that were not trending in social circles looked foolish by comparison. There was little excitement and few dramatic gains to discuss at dinner parties. The reward was delayed.

When the bubble burst in 2000, many of the fashionable stocks collapsed. Investors who had chased immediate gratification suffered heavy losses, while those who had stayed focused on a long-term plan recovered and benefited from years of compounding.

The lesson is simple. In investing, the choices that feel best right now are often the ones that deserve the most scrutiny. Excitement is not the same as value. The strongest long-term results usually come from decisions that feel boring in the present but sensible over decades.

items.title: When it comes to operating margins:- Nvidia=65% Apple=32% MICROSOFT=46% Micron=68% Meta=40%+ Price to sales fails as a metric when margins of the busi...
items.description_html:
When it comes to operating margins:-

Nvidia=65%
Apple=32%
MICROSOFT=46%
Micron=68%
Meta=40%+

Price to sales fails as a metric when margins of the businesses one is assessing are high.

Better metric to use is EV/Cash flow, Ev/FCF/

& Forward PE estimates which will help one to know PEG ratio that these businesses have.

Let us do one for example:-

Nvidia's PE=32 times
Nvidia's 1 Year Forward Growth estimates in EPS=100%

Effectively NVIDIA trades at a forward PE of 22 times for CY2027.

PEG=PE/GROWTH rates= 0.32. For 1 unit of growth theoretically, an investor is paying 0.32 times. Peter Lynch in his book has mentioned that he liked businesses which had a PEG of less than 1 times.

P/S is a vanity metric in a nutshell when it comes to valuing high margin businesses. Always check the

1. Longevity of growth
2. Margins if they are peaking out or not
3. The market expectations via reverse DCF
4. Peg ratio

Bubble or not- that is one's individual opinion :)


Sridhar Vembu: Price to Sales ratio for big tech (not price to earnings):

1. Nvidia: 20x

2. Apple: 10x

3. Alphabet (Google): 11x

4. Microsoft: 10x

5. Meta: 7.5x

6. Micron: 19x

As Scott McNealy of Sun Micro said back on 2002: "At 10x revenues, to give you a ten-year payback, I have to pay

items.title: Novak Djokovic's Discipline - "How much discipline? In January 2012, I beat Nadal in the finals of the Australian Open. The match lasted five hours an...
items.description_html:
Novak Djokovic's Discipline -

"How much discipline? In January 2012, I beat Nadal in the finals of the Australian Open. The match lasted five hours and fifty-three minutes – the longest match in Australian Open history, and the longest Grand Slam singles final in the Open Era. Many commentators have called that match the single greatest tennis match of all time. After I won, I sat in the locker room in Melbourne. I wanted one thing: to taste chocolate. I hadn't tasted it since the summer of 2010. Miljan brought me a candy bar. I broke off one square-one tiny square and popped it into my mouth, let it melt on my tongue. That was all I would allow myself. That is what it has taken to get to number one."

What a story of dedication and discipline!

Loved it!

items.title: RT Ritesh Banglani: Continuing the theme from yesterday, it baffles me that our instinct to solve every overcrowding problem is to curtail demand rath...
items.description_html:
RT Ritesh Banglani
Continuing the theme from yesterday, it baffles me that our instinct to solve every overcrowding problem is to curtail demand rather than build capacity.

China has built 2000 km of railway in Tibet, of which 1000 km is above an unbelievable 13,000 feet (Joshimath is at less than half that altitude). Yet we are litigating a daily quota on tourists instead of demanding an expansion of transport infrastructure.

Instead of blaming the tourists for wanting to escape the intense heat of the Indian plains, we should be asking why our vast Himalaya cannot accommodate 10x the number of visitors than it does today.


Himalayan Hindu: The obsession with breaking records has successfully turned the fragile Himalayas into the world's longest PARKING LOT. Joshimath is currently choking under a massive 25-30 KM long traffic jam. From Vishnuprayag to 15 km beyond, tourists are stuck in their cars for hours.

Almost 3 lakh crore

DIIs bought 3.84 Lakh cr

If DIIs and Retail money mainly through SIP book wasn’t there, this would have been worse than any ever fall we have seen

Time for Government to recognise this and reward this crucial capital

Assuming someone does a 10 year SIP continuously or does a 3/5 lakh rs SIP for many years, the government can give them income tax discounts, incentives, late filing permission and same week refunds (there can be tiers as per tenure and value of SIPs, strict checks should be there of course)

Corporates should also reward loyal employees with MF Units (that can be vested like ESOPs depending on their tenure and milestones)

Don’t get me wrong but seems as if FIIs are systematically attacking Indian markets on all fronts and we need to arm the domestic markets in all ways to withstand this!

And i certainly hope FIIs do come back but till then reward the capital that is supporting our markets!

items.title: Why making India self sufficient in Defence is important? This is one Powerful short story from the HBL's AGM below: -"One of the most amazing stories...
items.description_html:
Why making India self sufficient in Defence is important?

This is one Powerful short story from the HBL's AGM below:

-"One of the most amazing stories is the grenades. Grenade is something we see in all movies. You know, in the movie hero will pull the pin out of his mouth, which is a stupid thing to do, and throws it.

-What happens when you throw a grenade is, it has to take a few seconds before it lands and explodes. We had this attack on the Taj Mahal Hotel, I forgot the year, they call it 2611?

-Two soldiers from a special forces that were asked to get into the hotel and drive the terrorists out, two died. One died because when he threw the grenade at the terrorist in the corridor, it didn't explode on time. That fellow threw it back, it exploded near the person who threw.

-It came back. Okay, this can happen only in a hotel corridor. Second one died because it exploded too early.
So now, why this happened? Because they were using mechanical fuses. Or they call, some other, there's a name for this. It depends upon, pyro, they call
it.

-So the army wakes up again, and by this time, the defence R&D organization, ARD, was very helpful to us in getting us introduced to these customers because otherwise they're not authorized to talk. So we have developed fuses for grenades, and we are certified to produce grenades. We have got orders for grenades.

-So we are using our fuses in these grenades. And after that, we realized that there were a lot more. Avalanche protection, you need to throw some sort of
a shell into the snowbank, and that should also explode on time.

-One of you asked about proximity. Yes, we have developed proximity fuses, and the air defence fuses are proximity fuses, which is why they were so
difficult for the army to source. We have developed the proximity fuses.They have been tested intensively in the company, but now they're going to be certified by whoever has to do the certification.

-We have developed fuses for rockets launched by the Navy from ships. We have developed fuses for bombs dropped by the Air Force".

Not only Fuses. Indigenisation across defence products and building self reliance is important

items.description_html:
India’s Presidency of BRICS has come at a very pivotal time for the global economy. It was in this context that the BRICS Academic Mid‑Term Conference in Dehradun, on 29 May 2026, assumed a lot of significance and enthusiastic participation from across the BRICS member countries plus partner nations.

CareEdge Global and the Observer Research Foundation launched an important new report at the Conference: “Breaking convention: How CareEdge Global Ratings is redefining global credit risk assessment.” The report — unveiled by MD and Group CEO of CareEdge - Mr. Mehul Pandya; Vice‑Chancellor, Doon University, Dehradun - Dr. Prof Surekha Dangwal, and Vice President - ORF and Executive Director, BRICS Think Tank Council - India, Dr. Harsh V. Pant — lays out a forward‑looking framework that challenges traditional credit paradigms and addresses geopolitical, climate, and structural risks shaping sovereign and corporate credit today. The event also featured an excellent keynote address by Shri Shambhu L. Hakki, Jt. Secretary (MER) and BRICS Sous-Sherpa for India.

A proud moment for collaboration between academia, policy, and industry. Read the report to understand the next frontier in credit risk thinking.

#CareEdgeGlobal #ORF #BRICS #SovereignRisk #CreditRatings
